data.title: Thornburg International Equity Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

Let’s acknowledge the shared challenges faced by both portfolio managers and clients as we navigate today’s volatile market landscape. With headlines dominated by inflation concerns, recession fears, and tariff tensions, it is no surprise investors are feeling uneasy. Uncertainty causes doubt. Yet, it is precisely in these moments of uncertainty that opportunities emerge, especially in international markets.

For the past decade and a half, U.S. equities have taken center stage. But now, international markets—often underappreciated—are revealing compelling potential. As the U.S. contends with persistent inflation, China faces deflation and plans a massive stimulus, Japan moves beyond a decades-long deflationary environment, and Europe begins to assert greater economic independence, international markets offer a diverse set of investment conditions worth exploring.

While tariffs remain a topical issue for the U.S., they are far less of a focus among other regions. It’s important not to project the U.S. experience onto other economies—each operates under its own unique set of circumstances.

Market swings can be unsettling, but volatility is not inherently negative. For long-term investors, it can be a powerful ally. Volatility creates windows of opportunity—periods when patience, discipline, and a long-term mindset can pave the way for positive outcomes. During the recent bouts of market turbulence, We have been actively capitalizing on select opportunities.

Every economic cloud has a silver lining. Our team is here to help our clients navigate through uncertain waters—with perspective, conviction, a steady hand, and some grey hairs, too.

First Quarter 2025 Performance Highlights

Current Positioning and Outlook

Based on our fundamental, stock-focused research, we continue to see compelling prospects in select, high-quality industrial, financial and information technology stocks that have competitive advantages in their respective geographies, and in some cases, globally. These include companies in Japan, France and Germany, among other jurisdictions. We also have meaningful allocations to attractively valued opportunities among utilities and health care, given their strong fundamentals and earnings profiles.

The Trump administration telegraphed its intention to employ tariffs to rebalance global trade long ago. Our active positioning has enabled us to anticipate and navigate a variety of complex environments effectively. It is a basic part of our risk management process.

We have strategically managed tariff-related risks, intentionally avoiding excessive exposure to companies whose business models depend heavily on factors beyond their control, particularly shipping products into the U.S.

We invest from the bottom up but are always cognizant of the operating environments of our portfolio holdings. We are aware of macro, foreign exchange, and geopolitical dynamics, including tariff and trade-war risks. But we believe it is most effective to focus on the nature, durability and visibility of earnings, along with rigorous valuation analysis and, at times, cost-effective FX hedges. This is how our strategies have negotiated general market volatility over many years.

This high-conviction, yet diversified portfolio aims to deliver superior risk-adjusted returns through market and economic cycles by participating in market upswings, as we saw in the first half of 2024, as well as by protecting in the downturns, such as in the first quarter of 2025. We appreciate that broad market swings often cause share prices to deviate sharply from our assessment of business fundamentals and intrinsic value. We are always ready to take advantage of opportunities as they arise.

Our focus on a concentrated set of diverse international stocks that demonstrate high-quality management, solid fundamentals and a clear path to value realization, has served us well both in turbulent and fair-weather conditions, in the short and long term. This flexible and, we find, repeatable process should help us navigate the unpredictable policy and economic volatility in 2025.

data.title: EWU: UK Stocks Soaring In 2025, Decade-Plus Highs In Sight
data.description:
International stocks have made quite a comeback in 2025. The Vanguard FTSE All-World Ex-US ETF (VEU), the fund I use to track the performance of foreign shares, is up 15% year-to-date, dividends included. The S&P 500 ETF (SPY), by contrast, is higher by just 2%.

Outperforming VEU is the iShares MSCI United Kingdom ETF (NYSEARCA:EWU). I had a buy rating on the fund six months ago, and EWU is up 18% since then, beating the S&P 500 by 17 percentage points.

But global equities are not as much of a bargain today. According to Goldman Sachs, as illustrated below, many international markets now trade above the long-term average valuation. Still, I reiterate a buy rating on EWU. I see the valuation as reasonable, while new momentum in the fund could take it to its 2014 highs in the mid-$40s.

European Equities Remain Cheap, But Their P/E Has Risen

UK Leads on the Total Shareholder Yield View

EWU +19% in 2025, Strong Among the 45 Country ETFs Seeking Alpha Tracks

According to the issuer, EWU seeks to track the investment results of an index composed of UK equities. It offers investors targeted exposure to large and mid-sized companies in the UK and is used to express a country view. The fund invests in growth and value stocks of companies across diversified market capitalization. It seeks to track the performance of the MSCI United Kingdom Index, by using a representative sampling technique.

Despite sharp YTD alpha with EWU versus the S&P 500, it remains a small to moderate-sized ETF with just $3.1 billion in assets under management as of June 2, 2025. That’s just marginally bigger than the fund was at the time of my Q4 2024 analysis—yet another reason to be upbeat on the product, since it’s not as if investors are overly bullish or euphoric on EWU.

Its annual expense ratio is moderate at 50 basis points, while the trailing 12-month dividend yield is more than two percentage points above that of the SPX at 3.5%. Pictured above, the total shareholder yield with UK stocks is about the highest you’ll find around the world.

Share-price momentum has gone from lousy to lustrous—the ETF Grade in that category per Seeking Alpha’s quantitative scoring system is now a strong A. Risk levels are in check, too, despite intense volatility in March and April. Finally, EWU is highly liquid, given an average daily volume of almost two million shares and a median 30-day bid/ask spread of only three basis points, per iShares.

For an update on the portfolio, the UK ETF plots along the top row of the Morningstar style box, indicating its high exposure to large caps. That helps to keep the quality of the allocation high, but it also means there isn’t much in the way of size diversification. I’d also call out that ex-US small caps have performed very well this year.

For EWU’s valuation, the price-to-earnings ratio is low at 13.5, while the long-term EPS growth rate is moderate at 8.92%. That yields a PEG ratio of 1.50x. Compared to my previous analysis, the PEG has jumped a quarter point, which is significant.

EWU: Portfolio & Factor Profiles

From a sector view, EWU’s portfolio looks much different from that of the S&P 500. Financials is the largest weight at 22%, and Information Technology, which is more than 30% of the SPX, is less than a percent of the ETF. So, if we see cyclical and defensives outperform, with the Mag 7 US mega caps lagging, then EWU should continue to produce alpha.

Of course, many hinges on how the US Dollar Index (DXY) performs. Through early June, the DXY is down 8.5%. The EUR-USD pair has soared by 9.1%. That works to the benefit of investors in EWU since the fund is priced in dollars.

EWU: Holdings & Dividend Information

Unfortunately, not everything comes up roses for UK equities. Over the past 10 years of price data, June has been the worst month on the calendar. Moreover, the August through October stretch has been bearish, too. So, aside from solid July price action, seasonality is a headwind.

EWU: Bearish June Through October Calendar Trends

The Technical Take

With shares still attractive on valuation, but no longer dirt-cheap, the fund has a very bullish chart. Notice in the graph below that EWU rallied through the key $38 level that I pointed out last time. With the upside move, a measured advance to between $43 and $44 is now in play, based on the height of the multi-quarter trading range. What’s more, the long-term 200-day moving average is decidedly positively sloped, suggesting that the bulls control the primary trend.

Also, take a look at the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the chart—it has been hovering in a bullish spot, though it’s close to technical overbought conditions. With a high amount of volume by price below today’s level, there should be ample cushion if we see a protracted retreat.

If we were to zoom out the view to its entire history, we’d find that the 2014 high of $44 could easily be struck this year. By the way, $55 is the all-time peak from 2007.

EWU: Bullish Upside Breakout Targets the mid-$40s, Rising 200dma

The Bottom Line

I have a buy rating on EWU. The fund has soared since my previous analysis in December 2024, and I still see possible upside given the modest valuation, high momentum, and positive technicals.

data.title: Despite Tourism Risks, Trip.Com Strikes Me As A Good Buy
data.description:
Right off the bat, I have to acknowledge that I am a bit wary of the tourism sector in general, as high inflation and the ongoing trade war could eventually impact travel and leisure. Given that holidays are often planned months in advance, I believe this summer’s holiday season will be fine, but we may see cooling growth and spending come autumn, and especially in 2026.

That said, the Trip.com Group (NASDAQ:TCOM) strikes me as an attractive option, enjoying strong growth, and various tailwinds could potentially propel the company upward. My current worries over the tourism industry have made me hesitant to pull the trigger, but I do believe TCOM is worthy of a buy designation and may end up adding a long stake despite my trepidations.

Anchored in China, Expanding Globally

TCOM is actually the largest online travel agency in China and is headquartered in Shanghai. China’s economy has been a mixed bag as of late, but in some ways, that could offer some benefits for TCOM as many Chinese are shifting away from expensive overseas vacations and instead sticking closer to home, where TCOM is currently at its strongest. In 2024, domestic trips grew by nearly 15% to top 5.6 billion in China.

Outbound Chinese tourism has also been increasing rapidly since the end of strict COVID-19 protocols, yet as of 2024, outbound tourism has yet to fully recover from pre-COVID times. Projections currently peg domestic Chinese tourism spending to reach a record-breaking $968 billion this year, which bodes well for TCOM.

Another thing to consider is that Chinese households are sitting on massive savings. While the real estate crisis has hurt many Chinese households, savings remain high. Total household deposits, according to Reuters, exceed $22 trillion USD and World Bank data shows gross domestic savings regularly exceeding 40% of China's GDP. The Chinese government has been moving to suppress interest rates paid on deposits to spur spending, although so far, Chinese consumers remain reticent. If the Chinese government can figure out how to get Chinese citizens to spend, it’d bode well for TCOM.

That said, while the Chinese market offers a good foundation for TCOM, the company’s long-term goal is to expand international operations. Outbound Chinese tourism could help with that, and since international travel often results in higher budgets and more spending, it could drive more revenues and profits for TCOM.

For now, TCOM is emphasizing the Asia Pacific market according to statements from company leadership during the latest earnings call. Within the APAC region, the biggest direct competitor is probably Agoda, which is owned by Booking (NYSE:BKNG). Agoda has led the Asian market for years, and BKNG is no slouch, yet curiously, Agoda gets hammered when it comes to customer reviews. TCOM, on the other hand, performs much better (more on this later).

Anecdotally, I plugged in some July travel dates on both platforms for Kuala Lumpur, Bangkok, Hong Kong, and Tokyo. Prices and selection for both seemed competitive and on par with one another. Prices were also in line with what I saw on Airbnb. Granted, this particular analysis was far from scientific and was simply a quick glance.

AI and Experiences: A Two-Headed Growth Dragon?

TCOM’s leadership made sure to emphasize AI during their earnings call. While AI has become a bit of a buzzword in some ways, I do believe it could prove especially disruptive for client journeys that offer lots of upselling opportunities, including vacation and travel planning. I believe AI could be used to examine individual customer journeys and then tweak ads and promotional offers in a much more minute way than traditional mass data analysis.

Customer service is also crucial, but likely expensive for travel agencies like TCOM. Problems with bookings, basic questions, payment issues, and much more could quickly lead to high customer service expenses. I believe that travel companies like TCOM may ultimately stand to disproportionately benefit from AI agents and other services.

Another potential area for growth is in selling “experiences.” I’m not sure how much upside is here, to be honest, but Airbnb seems to have enjoyed success, and experiences at popular destinations have garnered hundreds and even thousands of reviews on Airbnb. TCOM may find similar traction. If nothing else, the company is demonstrating that it’s flexible and willing to try new ideas.

Customer Satisfaction Should Drive Adoption

Keeping customers happy can be especially difficult in the tourism industry. Unhappy customers are often the loudest customers, leaving negative reviews and expressing discontent on social media. Head to TrustPilot and Agoda scores just 1.2 out of 5, while Expedia (NYSE:EXPE) reels in the same 1.2 out of 5 score. Meanwhile, Trip.com scores 4.4 out of 5.

On TrustPilot, Agoda and Expedia are frequently accused of poor customer support and troubles with bookings. While you can certainly find unhappy customers for Trip.com, most reviews laud the company for effective customer service, easy check-ins, and savings. If Trip.com can continue to keep customers happy while also saving them money, the company should be able to snag market share from Agoda and Expedia.

TCOM Valuation and Fundamentals

It can be hard these days to find a company at a (historically) good valuation. P/E ratios have been trending up, and many investments, even in blue chips, have become at least somewhat speculative. This actually makes TCOM rather attractive with its P/E GAAP (TTM) of ~18.5, which is inline with the sector median.

Using Gurufocus’s DCF calculator to pin down a reverse DCF (with the discount rate set at 10% and the terminal growth set at 4%), free cash flows will need to grow by ~36% to justify the company’s current valuation. However, when we look at an EPS calculation with the same calculator, EPS growth will need to weigh in at just 9%. Given that the company is still in an early international expansionary stage, I believe free cash flows and EPS will trend upward.

Profitability is quite high with the gross profit margin topping 81% and the net income margin weighing in at 30.84%, according to Seeking Alpha's data. Year-over-year revenue growth is more moderate at 16.95%, but with expansion efforts underway, this could accelerate. EPS Diluted Growth (YoY) is also excellent at 51.63%. All in all, TCOM strikes me fundamentally as a good company.

My Reservations With Tourism/Leisure In General

Following the COVID-19 pandemic, travel surged globally. Part of this was due to pent-up demand and people looking to get outside of the homes they had, in some cases, been practically trapped in for months on end. The pandemic also resulted in high savings levels, which made it easier for people to afford vacations. Yet those savings have since run out.

Now, we’re seeing rising credit card debt, suggesting that consumers are turning to their credit cards to sustain their lifestyles. At the same time, credit card delinquencies have begun to tick upward. While not a crisis for now, both rising credit card debt and delinquencies could hint at future belt-tightening, especially if the unemployment rate should rise or household earnings stall or shrink.

Inflation could also pinch household budgets. While inflation has actually cooled in recent months, the ongoing global trade war could spur inflation in the not-so-distant future.

Even if consumers do start to tighten their belts, we likely won’t see the full effects for several months. Vacations are often planned months in advance, and many travelers have already paid for their trips. That said, I do believe tourism/leisure will be among the first things put on the chopping block if the economic environment deteriorates.

My Take-Away: TCOM is a Soft Buy, But Keep an Eye on Consumer Spending and Tourism

As a company, I believe Trip.com has plenty of room to grow, and positive customer experiences should propel success. Agoda/Booking.com and Expedia both strike me as vulnerable companies owing to negative customer reviews and minimal moats. While Trip.com currently seems focused on expanding in Asia for now, future pushes into other markets could spur further growth.

That said, I do have concerns over tourism and leisure in general. More people are living off credit cards, and delinquencies are on the rise. The ongoing trade war could further spur inflation and weigh down on consumer sentiments. This may result in would-be travelers skipping vacations in the future. While the 2025 summer will likely see high travel numbers overall, it’s important to remember that many vacations are booked months in advance. If consumers do tighten their belts, we may not see the full effects until late 2025 and into 2026.

If I do go long TCOM, I will keep a close eye on consumer spending and might make a quick exit if I find evidence that spending is coming under serious pressure.

data.title: Palantir: I'm Not Playing The FOMO Casino [Rating Upgrade]
data.description:
Investment thesis

I continue to believe that Palantir’s (NASDAQ:PLTR) fundamentals are stellar. However, several strong indications suggest that its valuation is not connected to fundamentals or any reasonable calculations. I have simulated various [even insane] FCF growth scenarios but none of them justify PLTR’s 300 billion market cap. At the same time, my previous bearish article looks not good as PLTR rallied by 50%+ since it was posted. This bubble keeps inflated by the FOMO, which makes timing the reversal almost impossible, and I do not want to be part of this casino. Therefore, I upgrade PLTR to “Hold” and will wait for a deep pullback from safe distance.

Recent developments

Palantir released its Q1 earnings on May 5, beating consensus estimates both from the revenue and EPS perspective. Palantir's quarterly YoY revenue growth accelerated to an impressive 39.3% from around 36% delivered in Q4 2024. Bottom line growth followed the impressive revenue dynamic as the company's EPS increased from $0.08 to $0.13.

It was high-quality EPS growth as it was achieved thanks to the operating margin expansion. The Q1 operating margin expanded from 12.8% to 19.9% on a YoY basis, which indicates strong operating leverage. This is a crucial bullish factor as it means that Palantir's FCF generation capacity is improving. As a result, PLTR's balance sheet becomes stronger and stronger every quarter. As of the latest reportable date, PLTR had $5.4 billion in cash with almost no debt. Thus, the company is extremely well positioned to invest aggressively in future growth.

The upcoming quarterly earnings release is scheduled for August 4. The Q2 revenue is forecasted by consensus to be $939 million, which will be 38.5% higher if compared to the same quarter of FY2024. The operating leverage improvement is expected to remain impressive as the EPS is forecasted to show a 53.8% YoY growth.

The notable indication that we might evidence peak revenue growth of Palantir is the fact that Wall Street analysts expect quarters after Q2 FY2025 to demonstrate consistent revenue growth rate decline through the end of FY2026. Revenue growth deceleration is rarely absorbed by investors with optimism, which is an indication that it is the peak PLTR optimism at the moment.

The fact that insiders continue selling the stock quite aggressively is another warning sign. In the summary below we can see that there are frequent sales, including the ones from the highest level executives like Alex Karp and Shyam Sankar. Such aggressive insider selling is rarely a bullish sign. It is highly likely that insiders might consider current valuation as extremely attractive for selling, not buying.

PLTR's technical setup is also far from being called attractive with its 65.5 RSI indicator. This is not far below the overbought zone and is also notably higher compared to the average level. A relatively high RSI indicator also means that expectations of the market are running hot. Thus, buying PLTR at the moment is quite risky from the technical analysis perspective as well.

Overall, the picture looks mixed. On the one hand, there is impressive revenue growth coupled with robust operating leverage. On the other hand, the next few quarters are expected to deliver revenue growth deceleration, which means that the current revenue growth rate has peaked. Quite high RSI indicator and aggressive insider selling also point to extremely high expectations of the stock market.

Valuation update

The "PLTR 5Y Avg." column in the table below suggests that Palantir's valuation ratios have been historically sky-high. However, current multiples are way above even historically high valuation ratios. Palantir's forward GAAP P/E ratio is 286, which looks absolutely unrealistic and out of this world. To illustrate, Cisco's P/E ratio peaked at 201 in March 2000 amid the dot-com bubble. Therefore, valuation ratios indicate that Palantir the "bubble of all bubbles".

Using even extremely aggressive growth assumptions for DCF analysis also does not justify PLTR's $300+ billion market cap. I have simulated various aggressive growth scenarios, and all of them suggested that the company's fair value is way below $300 billion. But let's go step-by-step and begin with the discount rate explanation. There is almost no debt in Palantir's capital structure, making cost of equity the most suitable to calculate the company's NPV. The cost of equity is 15.6% [rounded].

To make my DCF model more optimistic, this time I use unadjusted FCF margin, meaning that I ignore Palantir's significant stock-based compensation [SBC]. Therefore, the first-year FCF margin assumption is 30%. Due to PLTR's robust operating leverage, the metric is expected to expand by 200-bps per year. The FY2026 revenue assumption is $5.01 billion. The base-case scenario incorporates a 35.9% revenue CAGR with a very aggressive 6% perpetual growth rate.

The above DCF model is extremely aggressive, just look at it. Revenue is expected to increase by the factor of 6+ over the next seven years and the FCF margin to achieve 42%, a level not demonstrated even by well-known FCF champions like Meta or Google. Nevertheless, DCF suggests that Palantir's fair value is $162 billion, almost twice as lower as the current market cap.

The crazy part is that the company's fair value remains below $300 billion even if unrealistic 50% revenue CAGR is incorporated for the next seven years.

Risks update

I have to confess that I have not been very strong in timing PLTR’s ups and downs. I am not boastful, but I know that I had many extremely good and timely calls on other tickers, but PLTR is not one of them. Therefore, it might be a poor timing again and we are very close to a big PLTR selloff while I am upgrading the stock’s rating to “Hold”.

We see that growth expectations of investors are extremely high, which means that the Q2 earnings season might be a negative catalyst if PLTR fails to deliver on expectations or provide an upbeat guidance. Therefore, the reversal point might be approaching, and I might overlook it.

On the other hand, I might be wrong that I am not supporting the FOMO-driven thesis because there are indications that PLTR’s non-fundamental bull run might continue for a while as seasonality patterns suggest that June-July are historically very strong months.

Conclusion

To conclude, I upgraded PLTR to "Hold" despite vast overvaluation because choosing a side at this moment looks extremely risky. The FOMO-backed rally might continue, which is supported by robust historical seasonality patterns. On the other hand, with such a massive overvaluation, even a slightly unfavorable headline might cause a big PLTR sell-off.

data.title: Healthcare Tech And Innovation: Heart Monitors Improving
data.description:
Healthcare and technology innovation has far-reaching impacts that can truly transform and save lives. One area of ongoing focus is the growing challenge posed by heart disease. Heart disease is the leading cause of death in the U.S. Someone dies from cardiovascular disease every 33 seconds. These conditions encompass ailments like stroke, heart failure and, notably, atrial fibrillation (AFib). The latter is a rapidly growing issue, projected to affect up to 12 million Americans by 2030.

Diagnostics are vital to detecting heart problems, but traditional cardiac monitoring has had limitations. This note discusses the important advancements of the iRhythm (IRTC) Zio Monitor. iRhythm is a constituent of the Robo Global Healthcare Technology and Innovation Index (HTEC). This index identifies disrupting and emerging technologies in the healthcare sector.

Traditional Cardiac Monitoring Limitations

Many became aware of AFib through smartwatches and their EKG features. Yet, clinical diagnosis traditionally relied on methods like Holter monitors, event monitors, and 12-lead ECGs. These tools offered a high level of accuracy. But they often presented challenges regarding patient comfort and the limited duration of monitoring they covered.

For instance, Holter monitors, resembling an early iPod with five wires connected to the body, are typically worn for only 24-48 hours. This not only makes them uncomfortable but also means they might miss sporadic heart events. Event monitors, generally prescribed for 30 days, share similar discomfort issues and require patients to activate them upon detecting a symptom. Standard ECGs, while highly accurate, are performed in-clinic, meaning they can usually only diagnose an arrhythmia if it occurs during that specific visit.

Typical application of different methods of cardiac monitoring. From left to right: ECG, Holter Monitor and Zio Monitor.

A Modern Solution: Rhythm Zio Monitor

Recognizing this unmet need, back in 2009, iRhythm introduced the Zio long-term cardiac monitor (LTCM). The Zio monitor is engineered for up to 14 days of use, requires no obtrusive leads, and is secured to the patient via a simple patch, while at the same time improving performance. The monitor significantly aids in the earlier detection and management of heart rhythm problems.

Today, iRhythm’s Next Generation Zio Monitor is its smallest, lightest, and thinnest device yet. It incorporates a pioneering deep-learned algorithm, clinically proven to be as accurate as cardiologists in detecting 13 types of arrhythmia classes, along with specific beat types and runs.

Clinical Studies Highlight Zio’s Effectiveness

The CAMELOT study, published last year in the American Heart Journal, underscored the benefits of this approach. It revealed that for every 1,000 patients monitored with standard Holter monitors, there was an association with 180 more emergency department visits, 80 additional hospitalizations, and 920 more outpatient visits, when compared to those who used the Zio LTCM service. In other words, the Zio monitor’s earlier, more accurate diagnoses significantly reduced the need for subsequent medical interventions.

Building on this, iRhythm published the AVALON study in April of this year. This landmark real-world comparative evaluation involved 428,770 commercially insured patients with no previous cardiac monitoring history. The findings were compelling. Patients using the Zio LTCM service demonstrated superior outcomes across new arrhythmia diagnoses, reduced need for retesting, and fewer cardiovascular events compared to other ambulatory cardiac monitoring methods.

New Arrhythmia Diagnosis: Baseline Percentages (within 90 days)

The study population distribution where 36% of patients used LTCM, 36% used a Holter monitor, and 27% used an ambulatory event monitor (AEM). “Non-iRhythm LTCM” refers to other long-term cardiac monitoring services, which in the broader study included Bardy LTCM, Biotelemetry LTCM, Preventice LTCM, and “Other LTCM” providers.

Source: Comparative effectiveness of a novel P-wave-centric artificial intelligence algorithm with extended=wear Holter for new arrhythmia diagnosis: The AVALON study. American Heart Journal, 262, 101–111

As the table indicates, the study found that Zio LTCM users were more likely to secure a new arrhythmia diagnosis than individuals using alternative monitoring devices. In short, the Zio LTCM proved more effective at identifying cardiac issues. Compellingly, Zio LTCM users were also 1.95 times less likely to need retesting within 180 days when compared to those employing other non-iRhythm LTCMs.

Zio LTCM Performance Compared to Other Modalities

Zio LTCM Comparative Effectiveness for Arrhythmia Diagnosis, Retesting, and Cardiovascular Events. Likelihood ratios indicate how much more or less likely Zio LTCM is to result in these outcomes compared to specified monitoring modalities.

Source: Comparative effectiveness of a novel P-wave-centric artificial intelligence algorithm with extended-wear Holter for new arrhythmia diagnosis: The AVALON study. American Heart Journal, 262, 101–111

Addressing Gaps in Cardiac Care Access

These improved diagnostic capabilities are particularly relevant in addressing “cardiology deserts.” These are areas where patient access to specialized cardiac care is severely limited. Over 46% of all U.S. counties, home to 22 million residents, lack a practicing cardiologist. These regions are typically more rural, face socioeconomic disadvantages, and bear a higher burden of cardiovascular disease. This is exacerbated by the fact that AFib is estimated to be up to three times more prevalent than previously understood, affecting at least 10.55 million American adults.

iRhythm’s Growth & Global Expansion

With an established U.S. market penetration of over 30%, iRhythm is now extending its reach internationally. In May of this year, the company announced its launch in Japan, a country where 1.6 million ambulatory cardiac monitoring tests are performed annually. iRhythm is targeting further market opportunities totaling over 5 million tests across EU and APAC countries.

iRhythm’s innovative contributions are recognized by its inclusion in the ROBO Global Healthcare Technology and Innovation Index (HTEC). This index showcases a diversified portfolio of global companies at the forefront of disruptive and emerging technologies in healthcare. The Zio system shares this platform with other leading-edge technologies, including surgical robots, blood-based cancer testing, lab-grown organs, gene-editing therapies and many more ground-breaking advances in health tech.

HTEC is the underlying index for the Robo Global Healthcare Technology & Innovation ETF (HTEC).

It is provided as general information only and should not be taken as investment advice. Employees of VettaFi are prohibited from owning individual MLPs. For more information on VettaFi, visit VettaFi.

data.title: Alexandria Real Estate: Now Too Cheap To Ignore, With Just ~1% Annual Growth Priced In
data.description:
Simply put, Alexandria Real Estate Equities Inc. (NYSE:ARE), a REIT that leases lab space to life science companies, has become too cheap to ignore. The stock now offers a 7.5% dividend yield and has a forward price/FFO ratio of 7.6x. Further, when analyzing the valuation using a reverse DCF calculator (using AFFO instead of FCF), I found that almost no growth is priced into the stock.

Additionally, the dividend looks like it's safe, as it should be well-covered by both FFO and AFFO this year and beyond. This is despite the company facing short-term headwinds like declining occupancy rates and lowered guidance.

In this article, I'll explain why ARE stock gets a Buy rating from me, specifically for long-term income-oriented investors willing to tolerate short-term uncertainty. But I'll admit that the stock's current downtrend does not bode well for momentum investors. It may take some time before the company returns to an uptrend.

Fundamentals Are Still In Decent Shape Despite Headwinds

Here's the thing: Alexandria lowered its 2025 FFO per share guidance from a range of $9.23-9.43 to a range of $9.16-9.36. In other words, the midpoint was lowered from $9.33 to $9.26. Plus, the occupancy rate of operating properties in North America fell from 94.6% as of Q4 to 91.7% as of Q1's end. Investors punished the stock for this in late April after its earnings report, and the problem is that a slight decrease in FFO/share is expected this year, which means the growth trend is over for now, as you can tell by the historical FFO/share figures below.

Side note: I want to stress that the numbers below are what the company calls "funds from operations, as adjusted," which is not the same as adjusted funds from operations (AFFO), a metric that deducts recurring CapEx from FFO.

However, the overall fundamentals are still in decent shape. One important thing is that rent collections remain high, averaging 99.8% from Q1 2021 to Q1 2025.

Further, much of the tenant base is resilient, with 51% of ARE's rental revenue coming from investment-grade or publicly-traded large cap tenants. Not to mention, 91% of leases are triple-net, 98% have annual rent escalators, and 93% of them pass on CapEx to the tenant. This provides the company with high-margin cash flows (70% operating margin in Q1) that are set to last for a long time since the weighted-average remaining lease term was 7.6 years as of March 31, 2025.

As you can see below, many of the top 20 tenants, which make up 36.2% of annual rental revenue, are familiar names that are highly profitable.

That being said, if 51% of ARE's rental revenue comes from investment grade or publicly-traded large cap tenants, that means a large chunk -- 49% -- does not, and that's worth considering.

Capital Discipline and Strategic Capital Recycling

One thing to look at when analyzing a REIT is the cost of capital and its capital allocation. A May 2 article from Seeking Alpha contributor Sanjay Chandiramani, CFA, estimated the company's weighted average cost of capital to be over 9%, driven by a high cost of equity, while estimating that stabilized cap rates on new developments were closer to 6.6%.

The good news is that the company is being strategic with its capital. It isn't issuing equity at depressed valuations to aggressively fund new developments. The company is instead focusing on asset sales and share repurchases. The company's 2025 "guidance range for dispositions and sales of partial interests" is $1.45 billion to $2.45 billion, per page 6 of the Q1 earnings release and supplemental information package.

Looking at the guidance, some money will be used to pay down debt ($290 million in net debt reduction is expected), and it makes sense to conclude that some will go toward buybacks since the company repurchased $208.1 million worth of shares in Q1 and has $241.8 million left authorized under the buyback plan. The shares were bought back at an average price of $96.71, and the current price is near $70. Now's the time for accretive repurchases because the FFO yield is so high, so I definitely expect or at least want management to do so.

Cheap Valuation: Very Low Growth Is Priced In

To figure out how cheap ARE stock is, I ran a single-stage reverse DCF using a forward AFFO/share estimate of $7.28 based on analysts' estimates. See below.

In the reverse DCF, I used a 10.76% cost of equity, calculated using the CAPM model and a levered beta of 1.253 (per simplywall.st). With the current share price of $70.30, the model implies the market is pricing in only 1.168% annual AFFO/share growth in perpetuity starting from 2026. This is quite conservative, given the company's historical growth and the secular growth of the life sciences industry. Side note: the $7.28 figure has been discounted back to account for the 9-month forward estimate.

Apart from this, it's easy to tell that the valuation prices in a lot of downside, with a high dividend of 7.5%, a low forward FFO multiple of 7.6x, and another low forward AFFO multiple of 9.7x. The annualized dividend of $5.28 per share is well covered by forward FFO and AFFO estimates, with the forward FFO payout ratio coming in at 57% based on management's $9.26 FFO per share midpoint guidance, and 72.5% based on the consensus AFFO estimate. Such an elevated dividend with room to grow is not that common, and it makes the stock more attractive.

Overall, while the life sciences sector is facing short-term problems from post-COVID overbuilding and tighter VC funding, the long-term trend remains favorable. The US is still a dominant player in biotech R&D, and clusters like those ARE focuses on are unlikely to lose relevance.

Risks To Consider

One near-term risk is that occupancy is declining. As noted earlier, it fell from 94.6% in Q4 2024 to 91.7% in Q1 2025, so this is a trend worth watching in the longer term.

Another risk is political: "Make America Healthy Again" could lead to reforms that pressure large pharma profits. If these tenants reduce R&D spend and downsize as a result, ARE could be impacted. That said, the life sciences sector's real estate needs are sticky and specialized, which I think will make tenants less likely to relocate often, contributing to higher retention.

Also, as mentioned earlier, 49% ARE’s rental income is tied to smaller or private tenants, which could present risks if market uncertainty persists, as the biotech sector is dependent on venture capital funding and favorable equity markets.

Lastly, a risk is that analysts' estimates won't be met. This is certainly possible since there have been several downward revisions for FFO recently.

The Takeaway

Alexandria Real Estate appears to be undervalued for a REIT with a solid portfolio, long-term growth potential, a track record of growth, and historically resilient cash flows.

With a safe 7.5% dividend yield, a forward AFFO multiple below 10x, and a reverse DCF implying the market expects just over 1% annual growth, expectations are clearly low.

But the company continues to collect rent and is recycling capital prudently through asset sales and buybacks instead of dilutive equity raises at a low valuation.

There are risks to watch, like declining occupancy, political pressure on pharma, reliance on smaller tenants, and downward revisions, but these headwinds may be more cyclical than structural.

I think long-term income investors should consider the stock if they aren't concerned with negative short-term momentum, as it's clear that the valuation doesn't price in much growth.

data.title: CrowdStrike Earnings: Upgrading To Buy After A Healthy Pullback
data.description:
Investment Thesis

CrowdStrike (NASDAQ:CRWD) committed what many consider the ultimate sin for a growth stock: it missed revenue estimates.

Yes, the miss was small—but it fuels the bearish view that CrowdStrike’s growth isn’t what it used to be. And to make matters worse, the company didn’t raise its full-year fiscal 2026 revenue guidance.

That said, I’m starting to think this modest pullback could be an opportunity to buy the dip.

We all know CrowdStrike isn’t growing as fast as before. But what it lacks in headline growth, it now makes up for with strong free cash flow. The business is throwing off serious cash—even if you’re paying 64x next year’s free cash flow, not this year’s.

In sum, CrowdStrike is a resilient, well-run business. This quarter wasn’t exciting, but it doesn’t change the long-term story. That’s why I’m upgrading my rating from neutral to buy.

Rapid Recap — A Very Bad Call!

Since I turned neutral on CRWD, this stock has been plowing ahead.

Looking back, it was clearly a winner—and I missed it. Let’s just call it what it was: a bad call.

But here’s the thing—I don’t recommend it unless I’m confident. And now, I’m confident. So, I’m changing my rating to a buy.

CrowdStrike's Near-Term Prospects

CrowdStrike is probably the leading cybersecurity company.

They help other businesses stay safe from hackers. Instead of relying on a bunch of separate tools to protect different parts of their systems, companies can use CrowdStrike’s Falcon platform, which does it all in one place—from protecting devices to managing user identities and detecting threats.

Their big value is stopping cyberattacks before they cause damage, and making security faster and more effective, especially now that they’ve added smart products such as their Charlotte AI to automate threat detection and response.

Moreover, they are increasing the amount of cross-selling they do with customers so that they’re making more money from existing customers, winning bigger contracts, and seeing broader adoption of their subscription model, Falcon Flex.

Additionally, as we heard in the earnings call, they’ve already landed over $3.2 billion in Flex deals.

As you can see, there's a lot to be bullish about here. So, let's now discuss its fundamentals.

Fiscal 2027 Revenue Growth Rates Could Stabilize at 25% CAGR

Let me get straight to it—this quarter, CrowdStrike was up against a really tough comparison from last year. And the same goes for next quarter (fiscal Q2 2026). It’s not an easy setup.

But once we get past that, things start to ease. The year-over-year comparisons become much less demanding, which means revenue growth should naturally stabilize—even if the company doesn’t execute perfectly.

With that in mind, it makes more sense now to start thinking ahead to fiscal 2027.

Here’s how I see it: CrowdStrike will likely finish fiscal 2026 with around 25% revenue growth. And as it heads into fiscal 2027, it’ll be facing much easier comps. That sets the stage for the company to hit 25% to 27% growth without having to stretch too hard.

Given that setup, let’s talk about what the valuation looks like from here.

CRWD Stock Valuation -- 64x Next Year's Free Cash Flow

As an Inflection investor, I've always been drawn to CrowdStrike's strong net cash position. As it stands right now, CrowdStrike holds approximately $3.9 billion of net cash (including marketable securities). This means that, including the after-hours dip, about 3% of its market cap is made up of net cash.

As a reference point, I typically try to back companies with roughly 5% or more of their market caps made up of cash.

Moving on, CrowdStrike is guiding for fiscal 2027 free cash flow margins of more than 30%. Given that their long-term target points towards free cash flow margins of 36%, I believe that interpreting this as around 32% free cash flow margins next year makes sense, see below.

With this in mind, I believe that there's a path for CrowdStrike to reach about $1.9 billion of free cash flow in fiscal 2027.

Consequently, this means that investors are having to pay around 64x not this year's but next year's free cash flow.

I'm not going to claim that this is a cheap price tag for a leading cybersecurity company, but it's difficult to argue that this is particularly expensive either.

Basically, this is a case where the stock is expensive, but that's the going price for a high-quality business.

Risk Factors to Consider

CrowdStrike is not a cheaply valued stock. For now, I believe that investors will give a pass to this quarter's revenue miss. But if CrowdStrike misses next quarter's analysts' revenue estimates, I don't think investors will be as quick to dismiss this cardinal sin.

What's more, on the competitive front, CrowdStrike is up against big players like Microsoft (MSFT), especially in security for identities, as well as Palo Alto Networks (PANW), both companies determined to take back market share from CrowdStrike.

On top of that, there are many companies that were put off by CrowdStrike given its cyber IT outage in the past, but were tied up to long-term contracts with CrowdStrike, and are now looking to move away from the platform, which could plague its near-term growth rates.

The Bottom Line

So yes, CrowdStrike’s recent quarter wasn’t perfect—it missed revenue expectations and held guidance steady. But when I zoom out and look at the big picture, I see a company with serious financial strength, growing free cash flow, and a clear path to 25%+ growth in fiscal 2027.

It’s not cheap at 64x next year’s free cash flow, but great businesses rarely are. This is a well-run, cash-generative company that’s weathering a tough macro setup and still delivering.

I'm upgrading my rating to buy—I think this is a compelling opportunity for patient investors to get behind a cybersecurity leader at a moment of temporary weakness, particularly given its high visibility into fiscal 2027.

data.title: EWS: Singapore Stock Market Looks Less Attractive Than Its Wider Economy
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Singapore's stock market, as represented by iShares MSCI Singapore ETF (NYSEARCA:EWS) has done relatively well recently, more than doubling the return of the S&P 500 during the past year. Foreign investors may therefore be enthusiastic about seeking exposure an economy which is widely perceived to be one of the most innovative in the world. I would, however, suggest that a great deal of caution is warranted for prospective investors in EWS. This is based on its poor long-term record and relatively weak relationship with the performance of the country's broader economy, as well as a rather concentrated holding profile, consisting of less than 20 stocks.

Recent Performance

As highlighted in my opening remarks, EWS has done very well over the last year, with a 37% return versus just 14% from the S&P 500.

The three year record for EWS is also pretty strong, and a 53% return versus the 48% offered by the S&P 500 is particularly impressive when one considers that mega-cap U.S. technology stocks (which the S&P is dominated by) have been one of the best possible investments you could have made over the past few years. Any single country ETF which is able to outperform the S&P 500 definitely warrants further investigation.

Historical Performance

Unfortunately, upon scrutiny of the longer term results from EWS, investors may be disappointed. In my experience, one and three year returns are much more likely to be subject to swings in investor psychology versus a long-term historical view, which tends to offer more of an insight into the quality of the equities which underlie any country ETF. In the case of EWS, a 151% total return since inception (in 1996) versus 1400% from the S&P is very striking.

Market Versus Economy

On initial inspection, the very modest 151% total return from EWS over a period of almost 30 years is rather perplexing, considering that Singapore is a country which has grown at a very rapid rate according to metrics like GDP.

Indeed, in 1996, the year when the ETF was launched, Singapore's GDP was $96.3 billion. By 2023, the latest year for which the World Bank has data, the country's GDP was $501.4 billion. So over a 27-year period, Singapore's economic output has grown by around 420% in dollar terms (equivalent to a 6.3% CAGR), yet this has not been reflected in the company's stock market, at least as represented by EWS.

Singapore is also ranked as one of the most innovative economies in the world, and this is not a recent development. Back in 2011, it ranked third in the Global Innovation Index, and by 2024 still managed to rank fourth, beaten only by the USA, Sweden and Switzerland. Yet since the start of 2011, EWS has performed almost eight times worse in terms of total return versus the S&P 500. It is therefore very apparent that Singapore's innovation leadership has not translated into equity market strength.

Holdings

Understanding what EWS actually owns is the key to understanding the disparity between its performance and Singapore's broader economic success.

Considering some of the sectors outlined above is helpful in explaining the poor record of EWS versus the broad U.S. market. Neither financials (38%) or industrials (22%) are typically recognized by investors as offering particularly impressive long-term growth significantly above the rate of inflation. And a very striking absence in the table above is the total lack of technology stocks (in notable contrast with the S&P where 32% of holdings are information technology firms).

It's important to mention that the "Communication" sector does include Sea Limited (SE), a Singaporean company which owns Shopee (with a 55% market share in South East Asian e-commerce). However, this is a relatively new addition to the ETF given that it only IPO'd in 2017. With the exception of this 15% allocation towards one (quasi-)technology company, the other sixteen holdings are on the whole unexciting from a growth perspective. As far as tech goes, it is also important to note that a significant portion of its contribution to Singaporean GDP comes from private companies and startups, which can't be tracked through the ETF.

This is particularly well illustrated by the first and third largest holdings, together accounting for 29% of the fund. The largest holding is DBS Group, a bank established by the Singaporean government fifty-six years ago and the third largest is OCBC, another mature bank which has been around for almost a century. While they have strong credit ratings and are quite likely to be around in another hundred years, investors should recognize they are very unlikely to offer explosive growth.

When considering the list of holdings above, it is also very important to highlight the degree of concentration in the fund's top holdings, arguably exposing investors to an unnecessary degree of risk. The top ten holdings account for close to 80% of the fund compared with the S&P 500 where the top ten represent just 36%.

The total number of different stocks held by the fund is also shockingly low. While the table above shows 23 holdings, this also counts cash and derivatives held by the fund, and the number of equities held is therefore just 17. It is difficult to argue one is achieving diversified exposure to a country with such a small number of stocks. Even the equivalent ETF for neighbouring Malaysia (EWM), which has a much less developed equity market, holds 32 stocks. And versus a typical developed country ETF like iShares MSCI France ETF (EWQ), with 61 equity holdings, or iShares MSCI United Kingdom ETF (EWU), with 74 stocks, the comparison is even more striking.

Valuation

One consideration which might make the unexciting holdings of EWS more attractive would be a considerable degree of undervaluation relative to history. Unfortunately this is not currently in evidence. As shown in the graph below, the current 14.4x earnings multiple offered by EWS is actually quite high compared with the last ten years, where the average figure has been just below 13x.

Furthermore, as shown below, over a twenty year period, the average P/E was also around 13x, so it is difficult to argue that EWS is enticing on the basis of historical valuation.

Dividends

One positive of large cap Singaporean equities from the perspective of income investors is that they are quite good dividend payers. As shown above, EWS currently offers a yield of around 3.6%, which is clearly better than the 1.29% offered by the S&P 500. For me however, this is not a factor which would materially affect my rating on EWS, since I am interested in maximising total return.

Fees

You can track the S&P 500 with an iShares ETF (IVV) for an expense ratio of just 0.03%. To access Singapore's equity market via an ETF, however, the fees are considerably higher, with EWS charging 0.5% annually.

I think it is always useful to consider how much fees will consume capital over the long-term. For an expense ratio of 0.5%, over ten years, around 4.9% of your total return is lost to fees. Over twenty and twenty-five year investment horizons, the figures are 9.5% and 11.7%, respectively.

Conclusion

Singapore is an innovative, high-growth economy, yet seventeen large cap public companies seem to represent a rather poor proxy for this innovation, and I don't the ETF holding them warrants such a high expense ratio. Taking this together with the uninspiring evidence offered by its poor historical performance, I feel confident in assigning a sell rating to EWS.

data.title: SOXL: Benefits And Drawbacks To The Leveraged ETF
data.description:
Fast facts

The Direxion Daily Semiconductor Bull 3X Shares (NYSEARCA:SOXL) is a leveraged ETF in the semiconductor industry with a factor of 3x, launched on 3/11/2010. SOXL has a net expense ratio of 0.75%, a trailing 12-month yield of 2.04%, and is a large and very liquid fund, with $11.51B of AUM (assets under management) and a 3-month average daily dollar volume of $2.84B. It means about one quarter of AUM changes hands on average every trading day, pointing to the fund’s popularity among short-term traders. The issuer, Direxion, specializes in leveraged ETFs, with dozens of such products based on equity indexes and single stocks.

Strategy

SOXL is designed to magnify the daily performance of the NYSE Semiconductor Index, which is also the underlying index of iShares Semiconductor ETF (SOXX). Direxion gives a clear warning on SOXL and its bear sibling SOXS:

The funds should not be expected to provide three times or negative three times the return of the benchmark’s cumulative return for periods greater than a day.

Indeed, the daily leverage factor is a source of drift, a topic that I will develop in this article. The prospectus makes this warning more specific:

The Fund will lose money if the Index performance is flat over time, and as a result of daily rebalancing, the Index’s volatility and the effects of compounding, it is even possible that the Fund will lose money over time while the Index’s performance increases over a period longer than a single day.

The fund invests in financial instruments that, in combination, provide 3X daily leveraged exposure to the Index. The most commonly used instruments are stocks, swaps and futures contracts related to the underlying index. Keeping a daily objective requires to rebalance the portfolio on a daily basis. Direxion reports a portfolio turnover rate of 145% in the most recent fiscal year, excluding transactions on cash instruments and derivatives. The total turnover rate is not disclosed and is likely much higher.

Underlying index

The NYSE Semiconductor Index is composed of 30 stocks and is very concentrated. Indeed, the top 10 constituents, listed in the next table based on SOXX, represent 59.1% of asset value and the five largest positions weigh between 6.8% and 8.7%. Risks related to other individual companies are moderate.

Despite better performance, weaker trading volumes and excessive exposure to a single stock make the USD less attractive than SOXL for traders. Historical returns are irrelevant because these ETFs are designed for short-term trades, and traders focused on Nvidia can find single-stock leveraged ETFs issued by Direxion and other ETP providers.

Performance

SOXL has had an annualized return of 24.4% since 3/18/2010, outperforming the non-leveraged fund SOXX by 4.9 percentage points.

That’s a really bad drift, raising a big question:

Why and how do leveraged ETFs drift?

Leveraged ETFs often underperform their underlying index leveraged by the same factor. ETF decay may have four reasons: beta-slippage, roll yield, tracking errors, management costs. Beta-slippage is the main reason in equity leveraged ETFs such as SOXL. To understand it, imagine a very volatile asset that goes up 25% one day and down 20% the day after. A perfect 2X leveraged ETF goes up 50% the first day and down 40% the second day. On the close of the second day, the underlying asset is back to its initial price:

(1 + 0.25) x (1 - 0.2) = 1

In the same time, the perfect leveraged ETF has lost 10%:

(1 + 0.5) x (1 - 0.4) = 0.9

It is just the result of compounding leveraged returns. In a trending market, beta-slippage can be positive. If the underlying index goes up 10% two days in a row, on the second day, it is up 21%:

(1 + 0.1) x (1 + 0.1) = 1.21

The perfect 2X leveraged ETFs is up 44%:

(1 + 0.2) x (1 + 0.2) = 1.44

Beta-slippage is path-dependent. If the underlying index gains 50% on day 1 and loses 33.33% on day 2, it is back to its initial value, like in the first example. However, the 2X ETF loses one third of its value, instead of 10% in the first case:

(1 + 1) x (1 - 0.6667) = 0.6667

Without a demonstration, it shows that the higher the volatility, the larger the decay.

Moreover, the higher the leverage factor, the larger the decay. If we come back to the first example of this section, a perfect 3X ETF would gain 75% on day 1 and lose 60% on day 2:

(1 + 0.75) x (1 - 0.6) = 0.7

The 3X ETF is down 30% in two days, while the 2X ETF is down only 10% and the underlying index is unchanged. The good news is that a higher factor also magnifies positive drift in uptrends. In the second example (underlying index gaining 10% two days in a row), the 3X ETF gains 69%, while the 2X ETF is up 44% and the underlying index is up 21%.

(1 + 0.3) x (1 + 0.3) = 1.69

At this point, I think you have already understood why leveraged ETFs are not long-term investments, and why, more than ever, timing is everything. People who say that a leveraged ETF “will always recover one day” don’t understand slippage. Some will recover, some won’t.

SOXL drift calculation and history

There is no standard or universally recognized definition for the drift of a leveraged ETF. Mine is simple and based on the difference between the leveraged ETF performance and Ñ times the performance of the underlying index on a given time interval, if Ñ is the leveraging factor. First, let’s start by defining “Return”: it is the return of a leveraged ETF in a given time interval, including dividends. “IndexReturn” is the return of a non-leveraged ETF on the same underlying asset in the same time interval, including dividends. “Abs” is the absolute value operator. “Drift” is the drift of a leveraged ETF normalized to the underlying index exposure in a time interval, calculated as follows:

Drift = (Return - (IndexReturn x Ñ))/ Abs(Ñ)

“Decay” means negative drift. The next table reports monthly and annual drifts of 3X bull and bear ETFs based on the S&P 500, the Nasdaq 100 and the NYSE Semiconductor Index.

SOXL has a positive monthly drift, but an annual decay of -10.29%. The leveraged bear semiconductor ETF SOXS looks much worse with an annual decay of -28.77%.

Positive drift follows a steady trend in the underlying asset, whatever the trend direction and the ETF direction. It means positive drift may come with a gain or a loss for the ETF. Negative drift comes with whipsaw in daily returns. Both SOXL and SOXS have negative annual drifts due to frequent whipsaws in the semiconductor industry. Additionally, they both have positive drifts in one month due a strong uptrend and few changes of direction for the underlying index in May 2025.

The next chart plots the 12-month drift of SOXL over the last 10 years.

The 12-month drift has been negative 71.6% of the time and its time-weighted average is -4.08%. This is the average annual decay normalized to the underlying index exposure. As the leverage factor is 3x, the average annual decay of SOXL itself is -12.25% over the last 10 years.

Takeaway: who should consider SOXL?

SOXL is a short-term trading instrument for seasoned traders who execute strategies with clear and precise entry and exit signals, and who understand the fund’s behavior behind the leverage factor. For a large majority of investors, they are not equipped to use SOXL in a manner that would be beneficial and should know the risks associated with the fund. It's definitely not an ETF for all investors.

This article answers these three main questions about SOXL:

  1. Is SOXL meant for short-term trading or long-term investing?
  2. Are there unique risks to investing in SOXL?
  3. What type of investor is SOXL best suited for?

Editor's note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for education purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.

data.title: Even With Challenges, SiriusXM Is Too Cheap (Upgrade)
data.description:
Shares of SiriusXM (NASDAQ:SIRI) have been a poor performer over the last year, losing nearly a quarter of their value, amidst ongoing subscriber monetization pressure. It also needs to focus on reducing debt after its corporate simplification transaction last year. That said, the company has announced some new initiatives, which could spark incremental growth. After having been bearish for some time, I upgrade SIRI to a “hold” in November, thinking valuation had sufficiently compressed. That proved premature with the stock declining a further 18%. Given where valuation is and with updated financials, now is a good time to update on SIRI. I am moving to a buy.

SiriusXM is trying to manage two competing goals: maximizing the revenue it gets from its devoted customer base while also bringing more cost-conscious consumers onto the platform. Essentially, it wants to maximize users without pricing some options so low that higher-priced users trade down to lower tiers, which would reduce the company’s average revenue per user (ARPU). It is ultimately a difficult balancing act between these two priorities, but we are seeing some tentative signs of progress. That said, the business still has clear headwinds, and we are in the “getting less bad” part of a turnaround more so than the “getting better” portion.

In the company’s first quarter reported on May 1st, Sirius earned $0.59 with revenue falling 4% to $2.07 billion. One thing the company is doing well is reducing expenses. Even with revenue down 4%, adjusted EBITDA fell by a relatively modest 3% to $629 million. Its cost-cutting program continues to progress well, and Siri is on track for $200 million of annualized cost savings this year. In the most recent quarter, SIRI cut G&A costs by $3 million to $108 million, while sales and marketing were down 19% to $176 million.

Beyond benefits from lower operating costs, Sirius should enjoy a multiyear cycle of lower capital spending as satellite construction and launch expense should steadily decline after its recent modernization program. These savings will be about $50 million in 2025 and a further $125 million benefit next year, powering incremental free cash flow, even holding the rest of the business constant. I would note the company still has $450-500 of non-satellite capital investments annually.

The bigger question for the business is how subscribers and ARPU evolve over time. Looking at its biggest segment, in Q1, SiriusXM revenue was down 5% to $1.6 billion, primarily due to lower subscriber revenue with ad revenue flat at $39 million. Its average revenue per user (ARPU) fell by 3% to $14.86 as more self-pay subscribers are receiving a promotion. Gross profit was $937 million with a 59% margin, down from 60% last year. It has 33 million subscribers with 7.4 million subscribers on a free trial, down just slightly from 7.5 million a year ago.

Now, in March, the company pushed through a rate increase. Encouragingly, churn declined 18bps to 1.6%, despite this price increase. This suggests its customers are somewhat price insensitive. SIRI benefits from a highly devoted core customer base. On the earnings call, management highlighted that it achieved its highest quarterly satisfaction ever. Thanks to a lower churn rate, it lost just 303k self-pay subscriber loss in the quarter, better than the 359k burn a year ago. Subscribers are 2% lower than a year ago.

Now, lower churn despite a price increase is very positive. Conversely, despite this price increase, APRU fell from last year. Management does expect ARPU to improve over the course of the year as the full effect of the price increase plays out. Over the past year, there has been an increase in promotional activity, to reduce churn, which has offset the price increase instituted this quarter.

Going forward, the question will be whether SIRI can retain customers at their current tier even after raising prices or risk seeing consumers trade down. Sirius was notorious for quickly offering discounts when customers plan to cancel, which minimizes churn but also weighs on ARPU, a behavior it will need to methodically back away from. It now offers plans as a low at $9.99 that offer only music to plans over $25. At a May conference, management highlighted that it is not seeing meaningful trade-down activity.

Alongside this, SiriusXM will be rolling out an ad-supported tier with a below $10 price point. It will include a subset of music and talk programming. Last quarter, it generated $394 million of advertising revenue, about 90% of which comes from Pandora and other platforms, so SIRI has relationships with major advertisers that it can build upon.

As we have seen with Netflix (NFLX), a well-structured ad tier can be additive to the overall platform rather than cannibalize the customer base. SIRI will begin testing the product in H2, and I am optimistic that this can support subscriber growth, or at least stem declines. This tier will not include sports, and it is still not entirely clear how it will work technologically. For instance, its music stations do not have advertisements; will the ad tier miss certain songs and hear an advertisement instead. Particularly for live talk shows, this can be more difficult than for a streaming service where content is not consumed live.

While there will be some issues to resolve, I believe this can expand the pie of potential customers. Partially offsetting this, pending click to cancel rules would be a headwind as they will likely increase churn by making it easier to cancel subscriptions. Fortunately, the FTC has delayed implementation to July from May. I see this potentially risking 1-3% of the subscriber base if implanted.

Additionally, Sirius’s business does not have meaningful direct tariff exposure, since it is a services company. However, new car sales are a key part of its sales distribution with the company generally providing several months free on a new car. In March, we saw new car sales surge as consumers tried to front-run tariffs. With auto tariffs now in places, we may see car sales begin declining in coming months, which will likely be a modest headwind for subscription growth.

With the ad tier unlikely to be meaningful until mid-to-late 2026, I see subscriber growth worsening to about -3% to -4% before improving. Partially offsetting this ARPU declines should slow, and I expect revenue growth to be around -2% this year.

Back in November, I was forecasting $1-1.05 billion of free cash flow in 2026. SIRI reiterated guidance for $1.15 billion of free cash flow. I do see some downside risk to this forecast, given my caution on subscribers. Its guidance is for -1% revenue growth; still, I now expect at least $1.1 billion of free cash flow, a bit higher than my November expectation. The fact shares are lower speaks to increased concern about future tier switching and cannibalization.

Now, SIRI is carrying $10.5 billion of debt. That leaves it with 3.8x debt to EBITDA, above its targets of low-to-mid 3x leverage. To achieve this leverage target, SIRI needs closer to $9.2 billion of debt. After paying its 5% dividend, SIRI retains about $700 million in free cash flow, which should improve to $800 million next year given lower satellite cap-ex. As such, I believe it will achieve its leverage target by the end of next year, opening the door to meaningful buybacks in 2027.

As it progresses over its deleveraging over the next year, investors will start to have buybacks in sight, which should begin to provide more support for shares. In 2026, SIRI will generate at least $3.30 in free cash flow, giving shares a 15.5% free cash flow yield. Even assuming a 2% decline long-term decline rate, shares should not trade above a 12% free cash flow yield, as that still provides a 10% return for investors. That means SIRI should be trading at $27.5 over the next year.

That gives shares 29% upside plus a 5% dividend yield. A year ago, I was a sell on SIRI, which was right. Six months ago, I moved to a hold, which was early. Now, I am moving to a buy. I don’t believe the fundamental story is all that different—this is a business in a well-managed decline. However, the valuation has gone from treating it like a stock with growth prospects to acting like there are risks of a sharp decline, which I do not see as likely. As such, I would take advantage of this weakness and begin adding shares of SIRI.
